items.plain: A lab value that estimates how many neutrophils (infection-fighting white blood cells) are in the blood.
items.why_it_matters: Lower ANC means higher infection risk. Your care team may change plans or precautions based on ANC.

items.plain: A type of white blood cell that helps fight infection.
items.why_it_matters: Neutrophils are key to infection protection. Treatment can lower them, increasing infection risk.

items.plain: A condition where the blood has fewer neutrophils than normal.
items.why_it_matters: During neutropenia, fever may require urgent guidance. Follow your care team’s fever instructions.

items.plain: A blood test that measures red cells, white cells, and platelets.
items.why_it_matters: CBC results help the care team monitor infection risk, bleeding risk, and treatment effects.

items.plain: A measure of white blood cells in the blood.
items.why_it_matters: Low WBC can mean higher infection risk. Your team uses it to guide precautions and timing of treatment.

items.plain: A protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen through the body.
items.why_it_matters: Low hemoglobin can cause fatigue, weakness, or shortness of breath and may require transfusion.

items.plain: The percentage of blood that is made up of red blood cells.
items.why_it_matters: Low hematocrit can signal anemia and may relate to fatigue or weakness.

items.plain: Small blood cell fragments that help the body form clots to stop bleeding.
items.why_it_matters: Low platelets increase bruising/bleeding risk and may require a platelet transfusion.

items.plain: A condition where the body has fewer red blood cells than normal.
items.why_it_matters: Anemia can cause fatigue and may affect activity tolerance; the team may recommend treatment or transfusion.

items.plain: A lab value that measures lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell).
items.why_it_matters: Some therapies affect immune function; your team may monitor ALC to understand immune recovery.

items.plain: A tube placed into a large vein to give medicines/fluids, draw blood, or give blood products.
items.why_it_matters: It reduces needle sticks but needs careful care to lower infection risk.

items.plain: A small device under the skin connected to a catheter that goes into a vein.
items.why_it_matters: Makes long-term treatment easier. Watch for redness, swelling, pain, or fever.

items.plain: A long IV catheter inserted in the arm and advanced to a large vein near the heart.
items.why_it_matters: Used for longer treatments. Care includes keeping it clean/dry and watching for infection.

items.plain: A thin, flexible tube used to deliver fluids/medicine or remove fluids.
items.why_it_matters: Many treatments use catheters; knowing the type helps you follow care instructions.

items.plain: Given into a vein through a needle or catheter.
items.why_it_matters: Many medicines and fluids are given IV; ask about expected side effects and timing.

items.plain: A procedure where a needle is placed in the lower back to collect spinal fluid or give medicine.
items.why_it_matters: Used for diagnosis and/or treatment. Your child may need rest afterward.

items.plain: A procedure that removes a small amount of liquid bone marrow for testing.
items.why_it_matters: Helps diagnose cancer and check response to treatment.

items.plain: A procedure that removes a small piece of bone marrow tissue for testing.
items.why_it_matters: Provides detailed information that can guide diagnosis and treatment plans.

items.plain: Removal of tissue or cells so they can be examined under a microscope.
items.why_it_matters: Confirms diagnosis and helps guide treatment choices.

items.plain: Medicine that helps a child relax or sleep during a procedure.
items.why_it_matters: There are usually rules about eating/drinking beforehand and monitoring afterward.

items.plain: Medicine used to block pain and sometimes cause sleep during procedures or surgery.
items.why_it_matters: Your child may be sleepy afterward; follow instructions about food, activity, and safety.

items.plain: A scan that uses x-rays and computers to create detailed images inside the body.
items.why_it_matters: Used to monitor tumors and treatment response; may require contrast or stillness.

items.plain: A scan that uses magnets and radio waves to create detailed images inside the body.
items.why_it_matters: Often used to evaluate soft tissues; some children need sedation to stay still.

items.plain: A scan that uses a small amount of radioactive tracer to show activity in tissues.
items.why_it_matters: Can help detect cancer activity and monitor response to treatment.

items.plain: Treatment that uses drugs to kill cancer cells or stop them from growing.
items.why_it_matters: Can affect healthy cells too, which can cause side effects; your team provides supportive medicines.

items.plain: Treatment that uses high-energy radiation to kill cancer cells or shrink tumors.
items.why_it_matters: Side effects depend on the area treated; the care team will share a specific plan.

items.plain: Treatment that helps the immune system fight cancer.
items.why_it_matters: Side effects may differ from chemo; report new symptoms promptly.

items.plain: Drugs that target specific parts of cancer cells to stop them from growing or spreading.
items.why_it_matters: May have a different side effect profile; your team will monitor labs and symptoms.

items.plain: A procedure that replaces damaged bone marrow with healthy stem cells.
items.why_it_matters: Often requires special infection precautions and long-term follow-up.

items.plain: Receiving blood or blood components through a vein.
items.why_it_matters: May be used to treat low hemoglobin or low platelets and reduce symptoms or bleeding risk.

items.plain: Receiving platelets through an IV to help with clotting.
items.why_it_matters: Helps reduce bleeding risk when platelet counts are low.

items.plain: Receiving red blood cells through an IV to improve oxygen delivery.
items.why_it_matters: Can help with fatigue, shortness of breath, and anemia symptoms.

items.plain: Drugs that treat bacterial infections.
items.why_it_matters: During cancer treatment, infections can become serious quickly; antibiotics may be started promptly.

items.plain: A medicine used to prevent or reduce nausea and vomiting.
items.why_it_matters: Taking anti-nausea meds as directed can help prevent dehydration and keep nutrition on track.

items.plain: Medicines that reduce inflammation and are used in some cancer treatments.
items.why_it_matters: Can affect appetite, mood, sleep, and blood sugar; your team will guide monitoring.

items.plain: A higher-than-normal body temperature.
items.why_it_matters: During treatment, fever may signal infection and needs prompt guidance from the care team.

items.plain: When germs enter the body and cause illness.
items.why_it_matters: Cancer treatment can reduce the body’s ability to fight infection; seek guidance early for fever or symptoms.

items.plain: A sick feeling in the stomach that may lead to vomiting.
items.why_it_matters: Common during treatment; medicines and diet strategies can help.

items.plain: Forcing stomach contents out through the mouth.
items.why_it_matters: Can lead to dehydration; call if vomiting is persistent or your child can’t keep fluids down.

items.plain: Loose or watery stools.
items.why_it_matters: Can cause dehydration; your care team may recommend fluids, diet changes, or medicines.

items.plain: Having fewer bowel movements than usual or difficulty passing stool.
items.why_it_matters: Some medicines cause constipation; your team may recommend stool softeners or diet changes.

items.plain: Inflammation of the lining of the digestive tract, often seen as mouth sores.
items.why_it_matters: May make eating/drinking painful; mouth care plans can help reduce complications.

items.plain: Sores or inflammation in the mouth.
items.why_it_matters: Can affect nutrition and hydration; report pain, bleeding, or trouble swallowing.

items.plain: Extreme tiredness that may not improve with rest.
items.why_it_matters: Very common during treatment; tell the team if it limits daily life or worsens suddenly.

items.plain: An uncomfortable feeling that can range from mild to severe.
items.why_it_matters: Pain can often be treated; tracking when/where it happens helps the team choose options.

items.plain: Nerve problems that can cause numbness, tingling, or pain (often in hands/feet).
items.why_it_matters: Some therapies can cause neuropathy; report new tingling, weakness, or trouble walking.

items.plain: A change in the skin that may look red, bumpy, itchy, or irritated.
items.why_it_matters: Can be a medicine side effect or infection sign; report new or worsening rashes.

items.plain: Hair loss that can happen during cancer treatment.
items.why_it_matters: Often temporary; plan for comfort and scalp care, and ask about timing and regrowth.

items.plain: Eating more or less than usual.
items.why_it_matters: Treatment can change appetite; nutrition support can help maintain strength and healing.

items.plain: The identification of a disease based on signs, symptoms, and tests.
items.why_it_matters: Understanding the diagnosis helps caregivers follow the plan and ask the right questions.

items.plain: A way to describe how much cancer is in the body and where it is.
items.why_it_matters: Stage helps guide treatment planning; your team can explain what it means for your child.

items.plain: A decrease or disappearance of signs of cancer.
items.why_it_matters: Remission status helps guide next steps and follow-up monitoring.

items.plain: When cancer returns after a period of improvement.
items.why_it_matters: Relapse can change treatment plans; your team will review options and timing.

items.plain: An abnormal mass of tissue. Tumors can be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer).
items.why_it_matters: The type of tumor affects treatment and follow-up. Ask your team how it applies to your child.

items.plain: Not cancerous.
items.why_it_matters: Benign tumors may still need treatment depending on location and effects.

items.plain: Cancerous; able to invade nearby tissues or spread to other parts of the body.
items.why_it_matters: Malignant diagnoses usually require cancer-directed treatment and careful monitoring.

items.plain: The spread of cancer from where it started to other parts of the body.
items.why_it_matters: Metastasis may affect treatment approach and monitoring.

items.plain: A doctor who specializes in diagnosing and treating cancer.
items.why_it_matters: Your oncology team coordinates the treatment plan and supportive care.

items.plain: Care that helps manage symptoms and side effects and improves comfort and quality of life.
items.why_it_matters: Supportive care can reduce suffering and keep treatment on track; ask what supports are available.
